Also need to prefix relative URLs with ../ . The URLs are for images: social_image: mobile-social.webp etc. They are relative paths; we need to prefix with ../. So change to ../mobile-social.webp etc. Also cover_full_width etc. So add ../ before each. Also tags list: keep same. Now translate the prose. We need to keep the same paragraph breaks. Keep the asterisk and underscore formatting. Also there is a line: AI will surely follow this pattern.* Yes, it can process... The asterisk is after pattern.* maybe footnote marker. Keep as is. Also there are bold markers **...**. Keep them. Now translate. Let's produce frontmatter: --- language: English (maybe keep as English? The field is language; could keep as English. Probably keep unchanged.) title: "The Last to Think" -> translate: "Los últimos en pensar" maybe. Keep quotes. subTitle: Human or Machine? -> "¿Humano o máquina?" category: AI (keep) subCategory: Policy (keep) date: 2025-05-31 (keep) modified: 2025-06-02 (keep) tags: [ edtech, education, technology, history ] (keep) social_image: mobile-social.webp -> prefix: ../mobile-social.webp cover_full_width: banner-thinking-decay-wide.webp -> ../banner-thinking-decay-wide.webp cover_mobile: banner-thinking-decay-square-200.webp -> ../banner-thinking-decay-square-200.webp cover_icon: banner-thinking-decay-square-200.webp -> ../banner-thinking-decay-square-200.webp Now the body. Translate each paragraph. Paragraph 1: "Every generation discovers its technological bogeyman. Some new device emerges, and suddenly civilization teeters on the brink of idiocy. You could set your Apple Watch by these panics—assuming it has a charge. Lest we resort...". Translate. Paragraph 2: "Today's panic centers on AI, but before we hyperventilate about 12-year-olds who couldn't write an analog 'thank you' note yet can "write" a deep philosophical paper on the themes of justice and redemption in *The Brothers Karamazov*, let's check our historical receipts." Paragraph 3: "From CliffsNotes to calculators, each new technology has sparked fears of intellectual decay. In the 1940s, educators warned that CliffsNotes would make students lazy readers. In the 1950s, comic books were blamed for juvenile delinquency. The 1980s saw calculators branded as "crutches that will hobble a generation." The internet was once feared as the end of attention spans, yet it democratized knowledge and connected rural students to world-class resources. Each time, society adapted rather than collapsed." Paragraph 4: "Consider GPS—the doomsayers were technically right. Most people under 40 can't read paper maps. But instead of becoming wayward travellers, we gained the ability to navigate more often, in any city worldwide, all while avoiding traffic. Who is hand-wringing about the loss of map-reading skills now? No one. We adapted." Paragraph 5: "AI will surely follow this pattern.* Yes, it can process vast information and generate human-like text at inhuman speeds. No, it can't replicate human emotional intelligence or creativity. The challenge isn't preventing its use but **teaching its effective application**, building literacy around it. **Students need to understand** when AI amplifies learning versus short-circuits development. **Educators must redesign assessments** for an AI-augmented world." Paragraph 6: "We simply can't expect the old methods to work in this new landscape." Paragraph 7: "We survived calculators, CliffsNotes, and (so far) TikTok. We'll survive ChatGPT. The kids will adapt. I hope the adults don't take too long to catch up." Then

line. Footnote line. Make sure to keep bold formatting. Now produce final answer.
